sections.heading: These guidelines apply to competent authorities and to (i) alternative investment fund
sections.summary: Guidelines apply to specific financial entities including AIFMs, depositaries, UCITS entities, CCPs, TRs, and others.
sections.source_quote: These guidelines apply to competent authorities and to (i) alternative investment fund managers (AIFMs) and depositaries of alternative investment funds (AIFs), (ii) undertakings for collective investment in transferable securities (UCITS), management companies and depositaries of UCITS, and investment companies that have not designated a management company authorised pursuant to UCITS Directive (iii) central counterparties (CCPs), including Tier 2 third-country CCPs which comply with the relevant EMIR requirements, (iv) trade repositories (TRs), (v) investment firms and credit institutions when carrying out investment services and activities, data reporting services providers and market operators of trading venues, (vi) central securities depositories (CSDs), (vii) credit rating agencies (CRAs), (viii) securitisation repositories (SRs), and (ix) administrators of critical benchmarks.

sections.heading: ESMA will also take these guidelines into account when assessing the extent to which
sections.summary: ESMA considers compliance with EMIR requirements for Tier 2 third-country CCPs.

requirements.text: ESMA will also take these guidelines into account when assessing the extent to which compliance with the relevant EMIR requirements by a Tier 2 third-country CCP is satisfied by its compliance with comparable requirements in the third country pursuant to Article 25(2b)(a) of EMIR.

sections.source_quote: ESMA will also take these guidelines into account when assessing the extent to which compliance with the relevant EMIR requirements by a Tier 2 third-country CCP is satisfied by its compliance with comparable requirements in the third country pursuant to Article 25(2b)(a) of EMIR.

sections.heading: These guidelines apply in relation to the following provisions:
sections.summary: Guidelines apply to specific provisions of financial regulations including AIFMD, UCITS Directive, EMIR, etc.
sections.source_quote: These guidelines apply in relation to the following provisions: a) Articles 15, 18, 20 and 21(8) of AIFMD; Articles 13, 22, 38, 39, 40, 44, 45, 57(1)(d), 57(2), 57(3), 58, 75, 76, 77, 79, 81, 82 and 98 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2013/231; b) Articles 12(1)(a), 13, 14(1)(c), 22, 22a, 23(2), 30 and 31 of UCITS Directive; Article Articles 4(1) to 4(3), 4(5), 5(2), 7, 9, 23(4), 32, 38, 39 and 40 of Commission Directive 2010/43/EU; Articles 2(2)(j), 3(1), 13(2), 15, 16 and 22 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2016/438; c) Articles 25, 26(1), 26(3), 26(6), 34, 35 and 78-81 of EMIR; Articles 5 and 12 of SFTR; Articles 3(1)(f), 3(2), 4, 7(2)(d) and (f), 9 and 17 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 153/2013; Articles 16 and 21 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 150/2013; Articles 16 and 21 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2019/359; d) Articles 16(2), 16(4), 16(5), 18(1), 19(3)(a), 47(1)(b) and (c), 48(1), 64(4), 65(5) and 66(3)1 of MiFID II; Articles 21(1) to (3), 23, 29(5), 30, 31 and 32 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2017/565; Articles 6, 15 and 16 (6) of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2017/584; Articles 6, 7, 8 and 9 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2017/571; e) Articles 22, 26, 30, 42, 44 and 45 of CSDR and Articles 33, 47, 50 (1), 57(2)(i), 66, 68, 75, 76, 78 and 80 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2017/392; f) Article 9 and Annex I, Section A points 4 and 8 and Annex II point 17 of CRA Regulation and Articles 11 and 25 of the Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2012/449; g) Article 10(2) of SECR; h) Articles 6(3) and 10 of the Benchmarks Regulation and Point 7 of Annex I of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2018/1646.

sections.heading: These guidelines apply from 31 July 2021 to all cloud outsourcing arrangements
sections.summary: Guidelines apply to cloud outsourcing arrangements from 31 July 2021, with review deadline by 31 December 2022.
sections.dtia_summary: Cloud outsourcing arrangements must be reviewed by firms by 31 December 2022 to ensure compliance with these guidelines.

requirements.text: These guidelines apply from 31 July 2021 to all cloud outsourcing arrangements entered into, renewed or amended on or after this date. Firms should review and amend accordingly existing cloud outsourcing arrangements with a view to ensuring that they take into account these guidelines by 31 December 2022.

Where the review of cloud outsourcing arrangements of critical or important functions is not finalised by 31 December 2022, firms should inform their competent authority of this fact, including the measures planned to complete the review or the possible exit strategy.

sections.source_quote: These guidelines apply from 31 July 2021 to all cloud outsourcing arrangements entered into, renewed or amended on or after this date. Firms should review and amend accordingly existing cloud outsourcing arrangements with a view to ensuring that they take into account these guidelines by 31 December 2022. Where the review of cloud outsourcing arrangements of critical or important functions is not finalised by 31 December 2022, firms should inform their competent authority of this fact, including the measures planned to complete the review or the possible exit strategy.

sections.heading: These guidelines are based on Article 16(1) of the ESMA Regulation. The objectives
Competent authorities to which these guidelines apply should comply by incorporating
Through its ongoing direct supervision, ESMA will assess the application of these
Within two months of the date of publication of the guidelines on ESMA's website in all
In case of non-compliance, competent authorities must also notify ESMA within two
Firms are not required to report whether they comply with these guidelines.
A firm should have a defined and up-to-date cloud outsourcing strategy that is
A firm should:
sections.summary: Firms must assign responsibilities for cloud outsourcing documentation, management, and control within their organization.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires clear assignment of cloud outsourcing responsibilities to ensure compliance with legal requirements.

requirements.text: clearly assign the responsibilities for the documentation, management and control of cloud outsourcing arrangements within its organisation
allocate sufficient resources to ensure compliance with these guidelines and all of the legal requirements applicable to its cloud outsourcing arrangements

establish a cloud outsourcing oversight function or designate senior staff members who are directly accountable to the management body and responsible for managing and overseeing the risks of cloud outsourcing arrangements

sections.source_quote: 13. A firm should: a) clearly assign the responsibilities for the documentation, management and control of cloud outsourcing arrangements within its organisation; b) allocate sufficient resources to ensure compliance with these guidelines and all of the legal requirements applicable to its cloud outsourcing arrangements; c) establish a cloud outsourcing oversight function or designate senior staff members who are directly accountable to the management body and responsible for managing and overseeing the risks of cloud outsourcing arrangements.

sections.heading: A firm should monitor the performance of activities, the security measures and the
sections.summary: Firms must monitor CSP performance, security measures, and adherence to service levels on a risk-based approach.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires risk-based monitoring of CSP performance, security, and service levels for critical functions.

requirements.text: monitor the performance of activities, the security measures and the adherence to agreed service levels by its CSPs

sections.source_quote: 14. A firm should monitor the performance of activities, the security measures and the adherence to agreed service levels by its CSPs. This monitoring should be risk-based, with a primary focus on the critical or important functions that have been outsourced.

sections.heading: A firm should reassess whether its cloud outsourcing arrangements concern a critical
sections.summary: Firms must periodically reassess whether cloud outsourcing arrangements concern critical or important functions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires periodic reassessment of cloud outsourcing criticality based on risk, nature, and scale changes.

requirements.text: reassess whether its cloud outsourcing arrangements concern a critical or important function periodically and whenever the risk, nature or scale of an outsourced function has materially changed

sections.source_quote: 15. A firm should reassess whether its cloud outsourcing arrangements concern a critical or important function periodically and whenever the risk, nature or scale of an outsourced function has materially changed.

sections.heading: A firm should maintain an updated register of information on all its cloud outsourcing
sections.summary: Firms must maintain an updated register of cloud outsourcing arrangements, distinguishing critical vs. non-critical functions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires updated register of cloud outsourcing arrangements with critical/non-critical distinctions and details.

requirements.text: maintain an updated register of information on all its cloud outsourcing arrangements, distinguishing between the outsourcing of critical or important functions and other outsourcing arrangements

sections.source_quote: 16. A firm should maintain an updated register of information on all its cloud outsourcing arrangements, distinguishing between the outsourcing of critical or important functions and other outsourcing arrangements.

sections.heading: For the cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning critical or important functions, the
sections.summary: Registers for critical functions must include specific details about outsourced functions, CSP information, and risk assessments.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical cloud outsourcing registers must include CSP details, function descriptions, risk assessments, and financial data.

requirements.text: register should include at least the following information for each cloud outsourcing arrangement: a reference number; start date and renewal dates; description of outsourced function including data types; category of function; time-critical support; CSP details; governing law; cloud services and data locations; assessment dates; risk assessment results; approval body; sub-outsourcers; budget cost

sections.source_quote: 17. For the cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning critical or important functions, the register should include at least the following information for each cloud outsourcing arrangement: a) a reference number; b) the start date and, as applicable, the next contract renewal date, the end date and/or notice periods for the CSP and for the firm; c) a brief description of the outsourced function, including the data that is outsourced and whether this data includes personal data; d) a category assigned by the firm that reflects the nature of the outsourced function; e) whether the outsourced function supports business operations that are time-critical; f) the name and the brand name (if any) of the CSP, its country of registration, its corporate registration number, its legal entity identifier, its registered address, its relevant contact details and the name of its parent company (if any); g) the governing law of the cloud outsourcing arrangement and, if any, the choice of jurisdiction; h) the type of cloud services and deployment models and the specific nature of the data to be held and the locations where such data may be stored; i) the date of the most recent assessment of the criticality or importance of the outsourced function and the date of the next planned assessment; j) the date of the most recent risk assessment/audit of the CSP together with a brief summary of the main results, and the date of the next planned risk assessment/audit; k) the individual or decision-making body in the firm that approved the cloud outsourcing arrangement; l) where applicable, the names of any sub-outsourcer to which a critical or important function (or material parts thereof) is sub-outsourced; m) the estimated annual budget cost of the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.heading: For the cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning non-critical or non-important
sections.summary: Firms must define register details for non-critical functions based on risk assessment.
sections.dtia_summary: Non-critical cloud outsourcing registers require risk-based detail definitions.

requirements.text: define the information to be included in the register based on the nature, scale and complexity of the risks inherent to the outsourced function

sections.source_quote: 18. For the cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning non-critical or non-important functions, a firm should define the information to be included in the register based on the nature, scale and complexity of the risks inherent to the outsourced function.

sections.heading: Before entering into any cloud outsourcing arrangement, a firm should:
sections.summary: Firms must assess criticality, risks, due diligence, and conflicts before cloud outsourcing.
sections.dtia_summary: Pre-outsourcing assessment requires criticality check, risk analysis, CSP due diligence, and conflict identification.

requirements.text: assess if the cloud outsourcing arrangement concerns a critical or important function
identify and assess all relevant risks of the cloud outsourcing arrangement
undertake appropriate due diligence on the prospective CSP
identify and assess any conflict of interest that the outsourcing may cause

sections.source_quote: 19. Before entering into any cloud outsourcing arrangement, a firm should: a) assess if the cloud outsourcing arrangement concerns a critical or important function; b) identify and assess all relevant risks of the cloud outsourcing arrangement; c) undertake appropriate due diligence on the prospective CSP; d) identify and assess any conflict of interest that the outsourcing may cause.

sections.heading: The pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on the prospective CSP should be
sections.summary: Pre-outsourcing due diligence must be proportionate to function risks and scale.
sections.dtia_summary: Due diligence must be proportionate to function risks, including operational, legal, compliance, and reputational risks.

requirements.text: the pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on the prospective CSP should be proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the function that the firm intends to outsource and the risks inherent to this function

sections.source_quote: 20. The pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on the prospective CSP should be proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the function that the firm intends to outsource and the risks inherent to this function. It should include at least an assessment of the potential impact of the cloud outsourcing arrangement on the firm's operational, legal, compliance, and reputational risks.

sections.heading: In case the cloud outsourcing arrangement concerns critical or important functions, a
sections.summary: Firms must assess risks for critical functions including security, compliance, and operational impacts.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical cloud outsourcing requires risk assessment of security, compliance, operational, and reputational impacts.

requirements.text: assess all relevant risks that may arise as a result of the cloud outsourcing arrangement, including risks in relation to information and communication technology, information security, business continuity, legal and compliance, reputational risks, operational risks, and possible oversight limitations for the firm

sections.source_quote: 21. In case the cloud outsourcing arrangement concerns critical or important functions, a firm should also: assess all relevant risks that may arise as a result of the cloud outsourcing arrangement, including risks in relation to information and communication technology, information security, business continuity, legal and compliance, reputational risks, operational risks, and possible oversight limitations for the firm, arising from: i. the selected cloud service and the proposed deployment models; ii. the migration and/or the implementation processes; iii. the sensitivity of the function and the related data which are under consideration to be outsourced and the security measures which would need to be taken; iv. the interoperability of the systems and applications of the firm and the CSP; v. the portability of the data of the firm; vi. the political stability, the security situation and the legal system of the countries where the outsourced functions would be provided and where the outsourced data would be stored; vii. possible concentration within the firm caused by multiple cloud outsourcing arrangements with the same CSP.

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, the due diligence should include
sections.summary: Firms must evaluate CSP suitability for critical functions including reputation, resources, and security.
sections.dtia_summary: CSP suitability for critical functions requires business reputation, skills, resources, and security measures.

requirements.text: ensure that the CSP has the business reputation, the skills, the resources (including human, IT and financial), the organisational structure and, if applicable, the relevant authorisation(s) or registration(s) to perform the critical or important function in a reliable and professional manner and to meet its obligations over the duration of the cloud outsourcing arrangement

sections.source_quote: 22. In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, the due diligence should include an evaluation of the suitability of the CSP. When assessing the suitability of the CSP, a firm should ensure that the CSP has the business reputation, the skills, the resources (including human, IT and financial), the organisational structure and, if applicable, the relevant authorisation(s) or registration(s) to perform the critical or important function in a reliable and professional manner and to meet its obligations over the duration of the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.heading: Where appropriate and in order to support the due diligence performed, a firm may also
sections.summary: Firms may use certifications and audit reports to support due diligence.
sections.dtia_summary: Due diligence may be supported by international standards certifications and audit reports.

requirements.text: use certifications based on international standards and external or internal audit reports

sections.source_quote: 23. Where appropriate and in order to support the due diligence performed, a firm may also use certifications based on international standards and external or internal audit reports.

sections.heading: If a firm becomes aware of significant deficiencies and/or significant changes to the
sections.summary: Firms must review due diligence if significant CSP deficiencies or changes occur.
sections.dtia_summary: Due diligence must be reviewed or re-performed if significant CSP deficiencies or changes are identified.

requirements.text: if a firm becomes aware of significant deficiencies and/or significant changes to the services provided or to the situation of the CSP, the pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on the CSP should be promptly reviewed or where needed re-performed

sections.source_quote: 24. If a firm becomes aware of significant deficiencies and/or significant changes to the services provided or to the situation of the CSP, the pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on the CSP should be promptly reviewed or where needed re-performed.

sections.heading: In case a firm enters into a new arrangement or renews an existing arrangement with
sections.summary: Firms must determine if new due diligence is needed for renewed arrangements.
sections.dtia_summary: New due diligence is required for renewed arrangements based on risk assessment.

requirements.text: determine, on a risk-based approach, whether a new due diligence is needed

sections.source_quote: 25. In case a firm enters into a new arrangement or renews an existing arrangement with a CSP that has already been assessed, it should determine, on a risk-based approach, whether a new due diligence is needed.

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, the written agreement should
sections.summary: Written agreements for critical functions must include specific contractual elements.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical cloud outsourcing agreements must include function description, dates, governing law, financial obligations, sub-outsourcing rules, data locations, security provisions, performance monitoring, service levels, reporting obligations, incident management, insurance, business continuity, and audit rights.

requirements.text: the written agreement should include at least: a) a clear description of the outsourced function; b) the start date and end date, where applicable, of the agreement and the notice periods for the CSP and for the firm; c) the governing law of the agreement and, if any, the choice of jurisdiction; d) the firm's and the CSP's financial obligations; e) whether sub-outsourcing is permitted, and, if so, under which conditions; f) the location(s) where the outsourced function will be provided and where data will be processed and stored, and the conditions to be met; g) provisions regarding information security and protection of personal data; h) the right for the firm to monitor the CSP's performance; i) the agreed service levels; j) the reporting obligations of the CSP to the firm; k) provisions regarding the management of incidents by the CSP; l) whether the CSP should take mandatory insurance against certain risks; m) the requirements for the CSP to implement and test business continuity and disaster recovery plans; n) the requirement for the CSP to grant the firm access and audit rights; o) provisions to ensure data can be accessed, recovered and returned

sections.source_quote: 28. In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, the written agreement should include at least: a) a clear description of the outsourced function; b) the start date and end date, where applicable, of the agreement and the notice periods for the CSP and for the firm; c) the governing law of the agreement and, if any, the choice of jurisdiction; d) the firm's and the CSP's financial obligations; e) whether sub-outsourcing is permitted, and, if so, under which conditions; f) the location(s) where the outsourced function will be provided and where data will be processed and stored, and the conditions to be met; g) provisions regarding information security and protection of personal data; h) the right for the firm to monitor the CSP's performance; i) the agreed service levels; j) the reporting obligations of the CSP to the firm; k) provisions regarding the management of incidents by the CSP; l) whether the CSP should take mandatory insurance against certain risks; m) the requirements for the CSP to implement and test business continuity and disaster recovery plans; n) the requirement for the CSP to grant the firm access and audit rights; o) provisions to ensure data can be accessed, recovered and returned.

sections.heading: A firm should set information security requirements in its internal policies and
sections.summary: Firms must set and monitor information security requirements for cloud outsourcing.
sections.dtia_summary: Information security requirements must be set in policies and agreements and monitored for critical functions.

requirements.text: set information security requirements in its internal policies and procedures and within the cloud outsourcing written agreement and monitor compliance with these requirements on an ongoing basis

sections.source_quote: 29. A firm should set information security requirements in its internal policies and procedures and within the cloud outsourcing written agreement and monitor compliance with these requirements on an ongoing basis, including to protect confidential, personal or otherwise sensitive data.

sections.heading: For that purpose, in case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, and without
sections.summary: Firms must implement specific information security measures for critical cloud functions.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical cloud functions require information security measures including identity management, encryption, network security, API security, business continuity, and compliance monitoring.

requirements.text: ensure that there is a clear allocation of information security roles and responsibilities between the firm and the CSP
ensure that strong authentication mechanisms and access controls are in place
ensure that relevant encryption technologies are used for data in transit, data in memory, data at rest and data back-ups
consider appropriate levels of network availability, network segregation and processing environments
consider mechanisms for the integration of cloud services with the firm's systems to ensure security of APIs
ensure that effective business continuity and disaster recovery controls are in place
adopt a risk-based approach to data storage and data processing location(s)
verify that the CSP complies with internationally recognised information security standards and has implemented appropriate information security controls

sections.source_quote: 30. For that purpose, in case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, and without prejudice to the applicable requirements under GDPR, a firm, applying a risk-based approach, should at least: a) information security organisation: ensure that there is a clear allocation of information security roles and responsibilities between the firm and the CSP; b) identity and access management: ensure that strong authentication mechanisms and access controls are in place; c) encryption and key management: ensure that relevant encryption technologies are used; d) operations and network security: consider appropriate levels of network availability, network segregation and processing environments; e) application programming interfaces (API): consider mechanisms for the integration of the cloud services with the systems of the firm; f) business continuity and disaster recovery: ensure that effective business continuity and disaster recovery controls are in place; g) data location: adopt a risk-based approach to data storage and data processing location(s); h) compliance & monitoring: verify that the CSP complies with internationally recognised information security standards and has implemented appropriate information security controls.

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should ensure that it is
sections.summary: Firms must ensure exit plans for cloud outsourcing avoid business disruption and maintain compliance during transition.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires exit plans for cloud outsourcing to prevent disruption and maintain compliance during transition.

requirements.text: develop exit plans that are comprehensive, documented and sufficiently tested
identify alternative solutions and develop transition plans to remove the outsourced function and data from the CSP
ensure cloud outsourcing written agreement includes obligation for CSP to support orderly transfer of outsourced function and data

sections.source_quote: 31. In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should ensure that it is able to exit the cloud outsourcing arrangement without undue disruption to its business activities and services to its clients, and without any detriment to its compliance with its obligations under the applicable legislation, as well as the confidentiality, integrity and availability of its data.

sections.heading: When developing the exit plans and solutions referred to in points (a) and (b) above
sections.summary: Firms must define objectives, triggers, business impact analysis, roles, and test exit strategies for cloud outsourcing.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires exit strategy development including objectives, triggers, business impact analysis, roles, and testing.

requirements.text: define the objectives of the exit strategy
define trigger events that could activate the exit strategy
perform business impact analysis for the outsourced function
assign roles and responsibilities to manage the exit strategy
test the appropriateness of the exit strategy using risk-based approach
define success criteria of the transition

sections.source_quote: 32. When developing the exit plans and solutions referred to in points (a) and (b) above ('exit strategy'), the firm should consider the following: define the objectives of the exit strategy; define the trigger events that could activate the exit strategy.

sections.heading: A firm should include indicators of the trigger events of the exit strategy in its ongoing
sections.summary: Firms must monitor CSP services using exit strategy trigger indicators.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires monitoring CSP services with exit strategy trigger indicators.

requirements.text: include indicators of the trigger events of the exit strategy in ongoing monitoring

sections.source_quote: 33. A firm should include indicators of the trigger events of the exit strategy in its ongoing monitoring and oversight of the services provided by the CSP under the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.heading: A firm should ensure that the cloud outsourcing written agreement does not limit the
sections.summary: Cloud agreements must not limit firm's and regulator's access and audit rights.
sections.dtia_summary: Cloud agreements must not restrict firm's and regulator's access and audit rights.

requirements.text: ensure cloud outsourcing written agreement does not limit firm's and competent authority's effective exercise of access and audit rights

sections.source_quote: 34. A firm should ensure that the cloud outsourcing written agreement does not limit the firm's and competent authority's effective exercise of the access and audit rights and oversight options on the CSP.

sections.heading: A firm should ensure that the exercise of the access and audit rights (for example, the
sections.summary: Access and audit rights must consider criticality and risks of cloud outsourcing.
sections.dtia_summary: Access and audit rights must account for criticality and risks of cloud outsourcing.

requirements.text: ensure exercise of access and audit rights takes into consideration criticality and risks of cloud outsourcing

sections.source_quote: 35. A firm should ensure that the exercise of the access and audit rights (for example, the audit frequency and the areas and services to be audited) takes into consideration whether the outsourcing is related to a critical or important function, as well as the nature and extent of the risks and impact arising from the cloud outsourcing arrangement on the firm.

sections.heading: In case the exercise of the access or audit rights, or the use of certain audit techniques
sections.summary: CSP must provide rationale and alternative solutions if audit creates risks to CSP's environment.
sections.dtia_summary: CSP must provide rationale and alternatives if audit techniques create risks to CSP's environment.

requirements.text: CSP must provide clear rationale if exercise of access or audit rights creates risk to CSP's environment
CSP must agree with firm on alternative ways to achieve similar result

sections.source_quote: 36. In case the exercise of the access or audit rights, or the use of certain audit techniques create a risk for the environment of the CSP and/or another CSP's client, the CSP should provide a clear rationale to the firm as to why this would create a risk and the CSP should agree with the firm on alternative ways to achieve a similar result.

sections.heading: Without prejudice to their final responsibility regarding cloud outsourcing arrangements,
sections.summary: Firms may use third-party certifications and pooled audits to reduce audit burden.
sections.dtia_summary: Firms may use third-party certifications and pooled audits to reduce audit burden.

requirements.text: firms may use third-party certifications and external or internal audit reports made available by CSP
firms may use pooled audits performed jointly with other clients or by third-party auditor appointed by multiple clients

sections.source_quote: 37. Without prejudice to their final responsibility regarding cloud outsourcing arrangements, in order to use audit resources more efficiently and decrease the organisational burden on the CSP and its clients, firms may use: third-party certifications and external or internal audit reports made available by the CSP; pooled audits performed jointly with other clients of the same CSP or pooled audits performed by a third-party auditor appointed by multiple clients of the same CSP.

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should assess whether
sections.summary: Firms must assess adequacy of third-party certifications and audit reports for critical functions.
sections.dtia_summary: Firms must assess adequacy of third-party certifications and audit reports for critical functions.

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should make use of the
A firm should ensure that, before an on-site visit, including by a third party appointed
Considering that cloud services present a high level of technical complexity and raise
If sub-outsourcing of critical or important functions (or material parts thereof) is
The firm should notify in writing its competent authority in a timely manner of planned
The firm's written notification should include, taking into account the principle of
Competent authorities should assess the risks arising from firms' cloud outsourcing
Competent authorities should be satisfied that they are able to perform effective
Competent authorities should assess on a risk-based approach whether firms:
Where concentration risks are identified, competent authorities should monitor the
